coredata.dc:title: Sustainable packaging film based on cellulose nanofibres/pullulan impregnated with zinc-doped carbon dots derived from avocado peel to extend the shelf life of chicken and tofu
coredata.dc:creator: Riahi, Zohreh
Khan, Ajahar
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Shin, Gye Hwa
Kim, Jun Tae
coredata.dc:description: A cellulose nanofiber (CNF)/pullulan (PUL) based multifunctional composite film was developed for active packaging applications by incorporating Zn-doped avocado-derived carbon dots (Zn-ACDs). The incorporation of Zn-ACDs improved the interfacial compatibility and produced a dense cross-sectional structure of the composite films. The Zn-ACDs added film showed no significant difference in water vapor permeability and surface hydrophilicity compared to the neat CNF/PUL film, but the tensile strength and elongation at break increased by ~45.4 % and ~64.1 %, respectively. The addition of 5 wt% Zn-ACDs to the CNF/PUL matrix resulted in 100.0 % UV blocking properties, excellent antioxidant activity (100.0 % for ABTS and 68.0 % for DPPH), and complete eradication of foodborne pathogens such as Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313 and Escherichia coli O157:H7 after 3 h of exposure. The CNF/PUL composite film with Zn-ACDs applied to the active packaging of chicken and tofu significantly reduced the total growth of aerobic microorganisms without significantly changing the actual color of the packaged chicken and tofu for 9 days at 10°C. This study demonstrates that CNF/PUL composite films with Zn-ACDs are a sustainable and environmentally friendly option for protecting food from microbial contamination.

head.ce:title: Sustainable packaging film based on cellulose nanofibres/pullulan impregnated with zinc-doped carbon dots derived from avocado peel to extend the shelf life of chicken and tofu

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A cellulose nanofiber (CNF)/pullulan (PUL) based multifunctional composite film was developed for active packaging applications by incorporating Zn-doped avocado-derived carbon dots (Zn-ACDs). The incorporation of Zn-ACDs improved the interfacial compatibility and produced a dense cross-sectional structure of the composite films. The Zn-ACDs added film showed no significant difference in water vapor permeability and surface hydrophilicity compared to the neat CNF/PUL film, but the tensile strength and elongation at break increased by ~45.4 % and ~64.1 %, respectively. The addition of 5 wt% Zn-ACDs to the CNF/PUL matrix resulted in 100.0 % UV blocking properties, excellent antioxidant activity (100.0 % for ABTS and 68.0 % for DPPH), and complete eradication of foodborne pathogens such as Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313 and Escherichia coli O157:H7 after 3 h of exposure. The CNF/PUL composite film with Zn-ACDs applied to the active packaging of chicken and tofu significantly reduced the total growth of aerobic microorganisms without significantly changing the actual color of the packaged chicken and tofu for 9 days at 10°C. This study demonstrates that CNF/PUL composite films with Zn-ACDs are a sustainable and environmentally friendly option for protecting food from microbial contamination.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing populations and demand for nutritious and balanced diets are driving more attention to producing healthier and safer products in the food supply chain. Along with meat products, vegetarian and vegan diets rich in high-quality protein, B-complex vitamins, essential amino acids, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and acidic minerals are becoming the most widespread food sources worldwide [1,2]. Most foods are highly susceptible to oxidation and microbial contamination, requiring active preservation strategies to maintain nutritional value and extend shelf life by extending shelf life through sustainable packaging methods [2,3].

Traditional commercial food packaging is usually made from petroleum-derived plastics such as polypropylene (PP), polyethylene (PE), and polystyrene (PS) [4]. However, non-biodegradable plastic packaging pollutes the environment, so researchers are looking for eco-friendly and sustainable packaging as an effective alternative. In this regard, active packaging systems based on biopolymers impregnated with active ingredients (e.g., nanomaterials) have emerged as promising replacements for non-biodegradable plastics [5]. This packaging is mainly due to two basic components: an active layer (active compounds, i.e., antimicrobials, antioxidants, gas emitters/scavengers) and a barrier (polymer matrix) [6].

Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as promising members of carbon nanomaterials with photoluminescent properties because of their small particle size (< 10 nm). CD's unique qualities, including simple synthesis, low cost, good compatibility, high chemical stability, environmental friendliness, hydrophilicity, and low toxicity, make the CD a suitable candidate for food safety applications [7]. According to previous reports, fruits are rich sources of biologically active components (e.g., phytoestrogens, tannins, polyphenols, perseitol, and other phytochemicals), which makes them suitable for various pharmacological actions, including antimicrobial, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory activities [8]. Reusing kitchen leftovers to develop value-added products can greatly benefit the environment and food recyclability. In this context, the peels of fruits and vegetables are often discarded after consumption, but research is currently underway to recycle food waste to produce value-added products such as bioactive fillers (antioxidants, antimicrobial agents, etc.). As a result, the avocado peel, which is an integral part of the avocado fruit consisting of biologically active components, can be utilized to develop a variety of functional compounds such as CDs. Avocado (Persea americana Mill.) is a tropical and subtropical fruit native to Central America and Mexico and is commonly consumed due to its associated nutritional benefits to human health [8].

The chemical structure of CDs consists of a central carbon core and surface functional groups, and the physiochemical properties of CDs can vary depending on the type of carbon source and functional groups [9]. Because of this, the interconnection of heteroatoms and metal-doped core CDs can endow these CDs with new functions and expand their application range. However, biomass-derived CDs easily aggregate without good stabilizers, reducing their antioxidant and antibacterial potential. In this context, doping CDs with metal nanoparticles can be a good strategy to obtain stable and functional CDs without additional stabilizers [10].

Zn nanoparticles (Zn-NPs) have been considered multifunctional materials due to their antibacterial potential, piezoelectricity, near-ultraviolet emission, and optical transparency [11]. It has been widely discussed that adding Zn-NPs to biopolymer films can overcome the limitations of weak antibacterial performance, barrier performance, and preservation effect [12–15]. Therefore, doping CDs with Zn metal can be a good strategy to obtain stable CDs with excellent functional properties [11]. By doping, Zn-NPs are anchored to the CD surface, and numerous COOH and OH groups on the CD surface bond with Zn2+ to stabilize the final structure [10]. However, the application of metal-doped CDs for food safety is still in its infancy. Therefore, continuous research is needed to produce highly efficient and stable CDs that can be produced with reliable active ingredients. In addition, synthesizing CDs using food waste as a carbon precursor increases economic benefits by lowering environmental pollution.

Biocompatible macromolecules, such as polysaccharides, which can be obtained from plants, are generally employed in developing biopolymer packaging films due to their vivid physical properties and sustainability [16,17]. Pullulan (PUL) is a bio-based fungus-derived polysaccharide recognized for its high oxygen barrier properties and excellent film-forming ability [18]. It is a low-calorie ingredient and FDA-approved food additive widely used in the food field. Due to its high transparency, good oxygen barrier properties, thermal stability, and biodegradability, PUL has been used to manufacture edible packaging films [19]. However, due to its high hydrophilicity and low mechanical stability, PUL often needs to be synthesized with other biomass nanofillers or biomass polymers to improve its properties or realize multifunctionality. Cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) are derivatives of cellulose, one of nature's most widely used biological polysaccharides [20]. The high specific area and nanoscale effects of CNF lead to thermal stability and excellent barrier and mechanical properties, making it a good choice for use as a reinforcing material or other functional filler [21–23]. According to Rezaee et al. [24], CNF as a compatibilizer improved the tensile strength and compatibility of the films when blended with PUL. They found that blending different formulations gave acceptable uniformity content and dissolution rate. Although the elongation of the film was reduced due to the rigid nature of CNF, flexibility was improved by the addition of glycerol. Further, Bu et al. [25] reported that the composition of essential oils with PUL and CNF improves composite films' UV shielding, water resistance, thermal stability, and mechanical properties. Based on previous studies, PUL and CNF are highly biocompatible and contain many oxygenated functional groups to promote hydrogen bonding interactions in composite films and improve the film's barrier properties, hydrophobicity, and mechanical stability. As a result, this blend has become a viable option for food packaging.

The main objective of this study was to develop functional films based on CNF/Pullulan (CNF/PUL) integrated with biomass-derived ZnO-doped CDs for active food packaging applications. The effect of Zn-ACDs content on composite films' chemical structure, microstructure, and UV resistance properties was investigated. The mechanical properties and antibacterial and antioxidant activities of the composite films were also investigated. In addition, to explore the practicality of composite films in active packaging, composite films were applied to test the shelf life extension of chicken and tofu.

Fresh avocado, chicken breast, and tofu were procured from a regional market (Seoul, South Korea). The cellulose nanofibers (CNF) were supplied by Moorim P&P Co., Ltd. (TEMPO-CNF, length: 5–50 μm; width: 10–20 nm) (Ulsan, South Korea). The commercial pullulan with a molecular weight of about 4.3 × 104 Da was procured from Korea Bio Polymer Co. Ltd. (Bucheon, South Korea). The Korean Collection for Type Culture provided us with the pure culture strains of Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313 (L. monocytogenes) and Escherichia coli O157: H7 (E. coli) (KCTC, Seoul, South Korea). 2,2′-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), ZnO nanoparticles (average diameter of 20–30 nm), and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company (St. Louis, MO, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Zn-doped avocado peel-derived carbon dots (Zn-ACDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Avocado peel was used as the raw material in the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. For this, distilled water (50 mL), avocado peel powder (2.0 g), and ZnO nanoparticles (5 wt%, based on the avocado peel powder) were combined with gentle stirring at ambient temperature. The mixture was then poured into the cylinder coated with Teflon, placed inside a 100 mL stainless-steel reactor, and then heated to 180 °C for 6 h in a muffle furnace (DAIHAN Scientific Co., Ltd., Daejeon, South Korea). The reactor was cooled to 25 °C to get the yellow-brown solution mixture. The reactant solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm Whatman filter to obtain the Zn-ACDs.

A film solution was prepared by dissolving 2 g CNF, 2 g pullulan, and 1.2 g glycerol in 200 mL of distilled water with constant stirring at room temperature for 120 min. Then, various concentrations of Zn-ACDs (1, 2, 3, and 5 wt% based on the polymer) were added, and the mixture was vigorously stirred for 10 min. The solution was then cast onto Teflon-coated plates and dried at 20 °C for 48 h. The composite films were peeled off and stored in a 25 °C/50 % RH environment until further use. In addition, the control film was prepared without Zn-ACDs following the same steps. The fabricated composite films were designated CNF/PUL, CNF/PUL/CD1, CNF/PUL/CD2, CNF/PUL/CD3, and CNF/PUL/CD5 films.

The hierarchical morphology (size and shape) of the synthesized Zn-ACDs was measured using field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). For TEM analyses, test samples were prepared on carbon-coated copper grids by dropping aqueous CDs dispersion and then drying at 25 °C. Average particle size and size distribution were determined using ImageJ software (ImageJ 1.53 k Wayne Rasband and Contributors, National Institute of Health, USA). To determine the optical properties of Zn-ACDs, the absorption spectrum was recorded in the range of 200–600 nm at 25 °C using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea). Fluorescence emission responses were recorded in the 300–420 nm range (excitation wavelengths) at 25 °C using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7100 FL, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Maximum fluorescence emission response was obtained by varying the excitation wavelength between 300 and 420 nm in 20 nm increments. The scan speed and slit width were fixed at 1200 nm/min and 5 nm, respectively. Surface functional groups were analyzed using a Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FT-IR 4×, JASCO Spectroscopy, Tokyo, Japan) in the range of 4000 to 500 cm−1 at 32 scans with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

To determine the surface characteristics of CNF/PUL-based films, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU 8010, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) was used at an accelerating voltage of 5–8 kV. The main components and surface elements of the films were surveyed using an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) (No. 56756, Oxford instruments INCA, X.act, UK) [26]. The structural and chemical composition of the fabricated films were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Chelmsford, MA, USA). The mechanical properties of the composite films were measured using an Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5565 Instron Engineering Corp., Canton, MA, USA) according to ASTM standard test D882–88 (ASTM, 2009). For this purpose, tensile strength (TS), elastic modulus (EM), and elongation at break (EB) were evaluated using rectangular film strips (10 cm × 2.5 cm) attached to the machine and operated at a cross-head speed of 50 mm/min (initial grip separation of 50 mm). The thickness of the films was measured using a digital micrometer (QuantuMike IP 65, Mitutoyo, Japan) with an accuracy of 1 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antibacterial efficacy of CDs and fabricated films
ce:section.ce:para:
Zn-ACDs and the fabricated films were tested for their ability to quench free radicals by employing the DPPH and ABTS techniques [26]. An aqueous solution was prepared using ABTS and potassium persulfate, resulting in final concentrations of 7 mM and 2.45 mM, respectively. The mixture was then incubated overnight in the dark to generate free radicals. This mixture was then diluted with distilled water to produce an ABTS solution until the absorbance at 734 nm reached the range of 1.0 ± 0.05 (Control sample). Separately, a methanol solution of DPPH was prepared by dissolving 4 mg of DPPH in 100 mL of methanol. Then, different concentrations of ascorbic acid (as a positive control) and Zn-ACDs (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μ g/mL) or 50 mg of film samples were added to 10 mL of ABTS and DPPH solutions, and the mixture was stored in the dark for 30 min. The radical scavenging rate of the film was calculated following the equation below:
Radical scavenging rate
%
=
A c
−
A s
A c
× 100
where A
c and A
s are the ABTS absorbances of the control and test samples, respectively.

The antibacterial activity of the Zn-ACDs was investigated in terms of minimum inhibition concentration and minimum bactericidal concentration (MIC/MBC) values and diffusion method (agar well) against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. Agar plates were inoculated with evenly distributing bacteria at a concentration of 106 CFU/mL. Subsequently, a hole with a diameter of 8 mm was created on the agar surface, and 150 μL of Zn-ACDs solution (5 mg/mL) was added to the created hole. An agar plate with a hole and no nanoparticles was used as a control. The agar plates were then placed in an incubator at 37 °C for 12 h, and the presence of inhibition zones on the plates was observed after incubation.

MIC/MBC values were obtained using the 96-well plate microdilution method. Bacterial cultures were diluted to a concentration of 105 CFU/mL, and Zn-ACDs were diluted to a concentration range of 10–1000 μg/mL. 200 μL of the determined Zn-ACDs concentration and 50 μL of bacteria were added to each well. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, 30 μL of resazurin (0.015 %) was added to all wells and incubated for an additional 1 h. Wells that retained the blue resazurin color were considered to have values above the MIC.

The antibacterial activity of the composite films against L. monocytogenes and E. coli was measured using the total viable colony count method. After incubation of bacteria in sterile media, the cultures were diluted to 104–105 CFU/mL using the appropriate broth. The composite film samples (200 mg) were added to 20 mL of the prepared bacteria solution in 100 mL sterilized conical flasks, and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C with moderate shaking. For comparison, a flask with the same dilution and without a film sample was also prepared as a control group. 100 μL of the culture from each flask was taken and inoculated onto agar plates in triplicate every 3 h. The reduction in bacterial colonies was then calculated as a percentage to estimate the total viable colony count of each bacterial strain.

Surface color (L, a, and b) was analyzed using a Chroma meter (Konica Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan) to determine the apparent color of the composite films. The total color difference (ΔE) was estimated according to this formula:
∆ E =
∆ L
2
+
∆ a
2
+
∆ b
2
where ∆L, ∆a, and ∆b are the difference between each color value of the film sample and the white background plate.
Light transmittance of the composite films was measured using UV–visible spectrophotometry at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660) to evaluate the UV barrier and transparency of the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA) of the film
ce:section.ce:para: The water vapor permeability of the composite films was evaluated using the ASTM E96–95 standard procedure. The test film sample was sealed tightly on the WVP cup with 18 mL of water. The WVP cup in a humidity chamber (25 °C/50 % RH) was monitored every 1 h, and the WVP of the films was determined following Roy et al. [15].

The 25 mm × 120 mm film samples were put on the analyzer's horizontal stage. A WCA analyzer (Phoneix 150, Surface Electro Optics Co., Ltd., Kunpo, Korea) was used to record the contact angle immediately after dropping 10 μL of distilled water onto the film surface.

Real-time quality monitoring of a perishable food storage model consisting of chicken breast and tofu during storage was studied. 10 g of fresh chicken breast and tofu samples were inoculated with a 100 μL cocktail of L. monocytogenes and E. coli cultures ( ~ 105 CFU/mL) and then dried in a sterilized clean bench. The treated food samples were wrapped in the CNF/PUL-based composite films (7.5 cm × 7.5 cm), put in a sealed polyethylene bag, stored at 10 °C for 9 days, and checked quality changes at every 3-day intervals. The bactericidal potential of the composite films was monitored every 3 days by opening one bag from each group, macerating the contents, and determining the bacterial population using a method described in Priyadarshi et al. [27]. The packaging tests were carried out three times, and the average results were reported. The photo images of the indicator film were obtained using a digital camera (Samsung Electronics, Seoul, South Korea). Quantification of thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) was performed according to the method of Li et al. [28]. Briefly, 5 g of chicken and tofu samples were homogenized in 7.5 % trichloroacetic acid and extracted under constant shaking at 50 °C for 30 min. Afterward, the homogenized samples were filtered using filter paper, and 5 mL of the resulting filtrate was mixed with 0.02 M 2-thiobarbituric acid in a 1:1 (v/v) ratio. The mixture was then incubated in a water bath at 90 °C for 30 min. The solution was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the absorbance of the solution was measured at 532 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Standard curves were prepared with 1,1,3,3-tetramethoxypropane (MDA) ranging from 1 to 10 mM, and TBARS values were reported as mg MDA/Kg sample. The pH was determined from a 5 g sample that had been digested for 3 min in 45 mL distilled using a stomacher (BagMixer 400, Interscience, France). Then the pH of the solution was measured using a digital pH meter.

The quality characteristics of fabricated composite films (triple) and Zn-ACDs were evaluated, and results were reported as mean ± SD (standard deviation). Resultant experimental data/outputs were statistically analyzed, aiding SPSS-24 and Design-Expert software (IBM-SPSS, NY, USA and Stat-Ease, Inc., USA). Using Duncan's multi-range test, significant differences between study qualities were determined at the 95 % confidence level.

Zn-doped ACDs (Zn-ACDs) were synthesized using avocados as raw materials by a typical hydrothermal method. In hydrothermal synthesis, the carbon source undergoes a complex hydrothermal process, including carbonization, condensation, and oxygen reduction. The results of TEM analysis (Fig. 1(a)) validated that the developed Zn-ACDs are evenly dispersed and formed monodisperse spheres with a size in the quantum range (below 10 nm). The diameters of Zn-ACDs were confirmed by plotting the histogram as illustrated in Fig. 1(a). The synthesized CDs have a size distribution around ~2.6 ± 0.88 nm, which relies on the histogram fitted to a Gaussian model.

The results of FTIR (Fig. 1(b)) analysis verified that the Zn-ACDs surface possessed -OH, -COOH, and nitrogen-containing functional groups. The peak at 3241 cm−1 is ascribed to the -OH stretching; the peaks at 1667 and 1769 cm−1 attributed to the existence of CC and CO, respectively; the peaks at about 1391 and 2927 cm−1 were indicated to the CN and CH vibration modes, respectively [29]. For the recorded FTIR spectrum, some peaks may be slightly shifted or overlapped from their actual positions due to the stretching vibration -OH, CC, CH, and NH functional groups in the similar IR region.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were taken to analyze the prepared CDs' optical characteristics. As exposed in Fig. 1(c), the absorption spectrum of the Zn-ACDs exhibited sharp absorptions around 230 and 295 nm. The strong absorption peak at 230 nm was primarily caused by π- π∗ transitions of CC groups, and a sharp peak at 295 nm with a slight lump around 326 nm was indicated by n- π* transitions of C-OH, CO, and CN [30]. When exposed to 365 nm UV light, the Zn-ACDs' aqueous solution emitted blue luminescence due to the quantum confinement effect (Fig. 1(c), right inset). The carbon source and synthesis method determine the intensity of the color emission, a characteristic phenomenon of Zn-ACDs [31]. The CDs' maximum emission and excitation wavelengths were measured at 433 nm and 360 nm, respectively (Fig. 1(c)).

Fig. 1(d) exhibits the emission spectra of Zn-ACDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–420 nm, with an interval of 20 nm). Initially, the peak intensity of the fluorescence emission increased as the excitation wavelength elevated up to 360 nm, and it started to decrease with a further increase. When the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, the corresponding emission spectrum had a maximum emission intensity of ~445 nm. Then, a clear bathochromic shift (redshift) from 445 nm to 500 nm in the fluorescent peak was noticed when the excitation wavelength varied from 380 to 420 nm. It shows that Zn-ACDs have wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission behavior. The small particle size distribution and lower energy emission routes of Zn-ACDs could be responsible for the redshift of the fluorescent emission at longer excitation wavelengths [32]. The precise mechanism underlying CDs' excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence properties has not yet been fully elucidated.

The XPS result (Fig. 2(a)) displays that Zn-ACDs are primarily comprised of C (carbon), N (nitrogen), O (oxygen), and Zn metal as the main constitutional elements. Three distinct strong characteristic peaks at 285.6, 533.1, and 1023.1 eV match the binding energies of the C 1 s, N 1 s, O, and Zn, respectively. The binding energy for N 1 s is represented by the weak peak at 399.4 eV. Fig. 2(b) shows that the high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s can be split into four fitting peaks positioned around 284.3, 285.5, 286.1, and 288.2 eV, reflecting the C=C/C-C, CN, CO, and CO bonding structures, respectively [33]. Fig. 2(c) depicts the fitting curve for the N 1 s, which can be fragmented into three components peaks attributed to the bonding structure of (398.6 eV) C-N=C, (399.6 eV)N-C, and (400.6 eV)N-H/N-C3 groups [33,34]. For the deconvoluted spectrum of O 1 s, the component peaks positioned around 531.5 eV and 532.6 eV reflect the presence of CO and CO groups, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(d). Further, a weak peak at 529.1 eV is indicated by the chemical bonding between oxygen and Zn rather than contamination or adsorption of Zn on the surface of Zn-ACDs [35]. Fig. 2(e) illustrates that the deconvoluted Zn 2p spectrum possessed two peaks located around 1020.5 and 1043.9 eV indicated to the Zn 2p core levels with splitting of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. Therefore, the XPS spectrum analysis validated the successful doping of Zn to the surface of ACDs [35].

In addition, Fig. 3(a) shows the appearance of the fabricated CNF/PUL-based composite films. The neat CNF/PUL film was transparent and colorless. However, the composite films appeared light brown when Zn-ACDs were incorporated with the CNF/PUL polymer matrix due to the yellowish-brown color of Zn-ACDs. The color of all composite films was uniform, indicating that the CDs were evenly distributed throughout the CNF/PUL blend matrix. It can be clearly seen that the yellow color of the proposed films intensifies as the Zn-ACDs content increases from 1 to 5 wt%. Therefore, it was important to assess the color and optical specifications of the fabricated films. As depicted in Table 1 , the integration of Zn-ACDs significantly (p < 0.05) decreased the brightness (L) and increased the yellowness (b) of the CNF/PUL film. The incorporation of varied concentrations of CDs had a significant (p < 0.05) effect on the changes in color values. Therefore, the overall color difference (ΔE) of composite films was significantly (p < 0.05) increased.

The surface appearance morphology of the CNF/PUL-based composite films with different Zn-ACDs contents was studied by FE-SEM analysis. Fig. 3(b) shows the surface morphology of the fabricated CNF/PUL/CD1, CNF/PUL/CD2, CNF/PUL/CD3, and CNF/PUL/CD5 composite films. The morphology of the neat CNF/PUL film exhibited an intact appearance without cavities and cracks. Adding 1 to 3 wt% CDs did not impact the surface appearance of the fabricated films, as no visible cracks or ruptures were present, indicating that Zn-ACDs have good compatibility with the CNF/PUL matrix. The microstructure of the CNF/PUL/CD2 composite film appeared to be considerably denser than the control film, whereas the CNF/PUL/CD3 composite film exhibited a more rigid and smooth appearance, which may be related to the higher concentration of CDs. However, adding 5 wt% CDs results in a smoother surface of the CNF/PUL/CD5, possibly due to the increased Zn-ACDs concentration in the CNF/PUL/CD composite film.

The cross-sectional morphology of the neat CNF/PUL film was rough and exhibited a multilayer structure with wide cavities (Fig. 3(c)). As the Zn-ACDs content increased from 1 to 5 wt%, the number of layer pores and cavities tended to decrease, and the shape of the cross-section tended to become dense and smooth. The number of pores in the composite film's cross-section was the smallest when the CD content was 5 wt%. After incorporation, Zn-ACDs can cross-link with the CNF/PUL matrix due to chemical interactions. The composite films eventually become denser because Zn-ACDs have a lot of active groups on their surface, such as amino hydroxyl groups that can form hydrogen bonds with the polymer matrix and the volume-occupied space that can fill the pores.

Fig. 3d-f shows the energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) data to determine the composition of constitutional elements on the surface of the neat CNF/PUL and the CNF/PUL/CD5 film. Elemental mapping shows the distribution of carbon and oxygen, the major elements constituting the CNF/PUL matrix. However, the uniform distribution of additional elements such as Zn and N (nitrogen) in the CNF/PUL/CD5 film confirms that the CDs were successfully composited as a binary-polymer matrix (Fig. 3d & e). Additionally, the EDS spectra also confirm the occurrence of carbon and oxygen as the constitutional elements of the CNF/PUL backbone (Fig. 3f). However, additional elements of Zn and N were found in the CNF/PUL/CDs5 film, confirming the presence of Zn-ACDs in the CNF/PUL film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characteristics of the CNF/PUL/CD films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4(a) displays the optical transmittance response of the CNF/PUL/CD composite films from 200 to 800 nm wavelength. The light transmittance in the UV region (at 280 nm) and visible light region (660 nm) of the fabricated films are also shown in Table 1. The UV transmittance of the CNF/PUL/CD composite films was much lower than the neat CNF/PUL film. The composite films have a good UV shielding performance over the entire UV range by incorporating Zn-ACDs. The CNF/PUL film showed high transparency with 86 % of T660 and significantly (p < 0.05) decreased to 59.6 % by incorporating 5 wt% Zn-ACDs.

On the other hand, the incorporation of CDs revealed a sharp decrease in UV-light transmittance T280 from 69.9 % to 0.2 %, possibly due to the polyphenolic compounds found in the Zn-ACDs. Based on the outcomes, it is possible to say that adding CDs enhances the UV barrier property while maintaining transparency. Therefore, the UV-blocking analysis suggested that the CNF/PUL/CD composite films are expected to have broad prospects in food packaging.

The FT-IR spectra of CNF/PUL-based films impregnated with various concentrations of Zn-ACDs are displayed in Fig. 4(b). In all CNF/PUL composite films, the stretching mode of O-H/N-H can be displayed as a broad absorption peak at 3000–3600 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CH assigned a distinctive absorption at 2901 cm−1, and the CO stretching vibration attributed the characteristic absorption at 1606 cm−1. The absorption at 1155 cm−1 was identified as the pullulan glycosidic stretching mode [36]. The CO vibration of CNF and PUL indicates the peak at 1018 cm−1 [36]. When Zn-ACDs were added to the polymer blend, the spectra of the CNF/PUL/CD1, CNF/PUL/CD2, CNF/PUL/CD3, and CNF/PUL/CD5 films displayed the characteristic peaks of CNF and PUL in proportion to the ratio of the two components, with a slight difference in the intensity of peaks in the 1500–1700 cm−1 range related to carbonyl moieties, indicating that electrostatic interactions account for most of these groups' interactions [37]. Additionally, FTIR spectra of CNF/PUL films incorporated with Zn-ACDs films show a broadening of the peak around 3150–3400 cm−1 and slight shifts in the peak positions of the participating groups compared to those of a neat film, suggesting that intermolecular hydrogen bonding between OH groups of the CNF/PUL and CD also contributes to the complex formation [38,39].

Overall, the FTIR spectra of the nanocomposite films were matched to that of the neat CNF/PUL film and had a similar pattern with their characteristic peaks except for a slight change in position and intensity of peaks brought on by the addition of Zn-ACDs, which confirms the successful incorporation of Zn-ACDs in the polymer blend. The overlapping CH, OH, CC, and CO stretching vibration in almost the same IR region was the cause of the minimal change in the IR spectra after the incorporation of CDs. Therefore, it can be inferred that the intensity of observed peaks in the spectra of the CD-added films remains unchanged with a slight shift in the frequency regions, supporting the uniform incorporation and reasonable compatibility of the Zn-ACDs with the CNF/PUL polymer matrix.

Packaging materials must have sufficient mechanical stability to retain structural integrity and avert packing failure [9,36]. The mechanical parameters of the CNF/PUL/CD films are summarized in Table 2 . The neat CNF/PUL film's thickness was 28.6 μm, which slightly increased to 38.8 μm, depending on the contents of the CDs. The presence of polyphenols, carbonyls, and nitrogen structure compounds in Zn-ACDs may cause an increase in the thickness, as they inhabit the interchain spaces in the polymer backbone and cause them to move apart themselves [40]. TS and EB of the neat CNF/PUL film were 46.3 MPa and 13.1 %, respectively, and pointedly (p < 0.05) improved with the loading of Zn-ACDs. The TS and EB of the CNF/PUL/CD5 were 67.3 MPa and 21.5 %, respectively, which is improved by 45.4 % of TS and 64.1 % of EB compared to the neat CNF/PUL film. The Zn-ACDs integrated into the composite film were present as reinforcers, and interactions between the CNF/PUL and CDs molecules, such as hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups, preserve the rigidity of the composite structure. Substantial and strong hydrogen bonds between Zn-ACDs and the polymer backbone may require more energy to break [41]. As a result, incorporating CDs increased the mechanical stability of the CNF/PUL/CD films. Similar effects of additives on the mechanical characteristics of several films made of biopolymers have been previously discussed [42,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: The WVP and WCA of the CNF/PUL/CD films are also shown in Table 2. The WVP of the neat CNF/PUL film was 4.3 × 10−10 g·m/m2·Pa·s. The incorporation of the Zn-ACDs did not significantly (p > 0.05) change the WVP of the CNF/PUL-based films. But the WVP of the CNF/PUL composite films was slightly increased in the high concentration of CDs because of the hydrophilic behavior of Zn-ACDs. CDs loading condenses the WVP of the composite films, which can be ascribed to the creation of a complicated pathway of water vapor diffusion by the water vapor-permeable hydrophilic CDs [36]. Despite creating H-bonds, the quantum-sized Zn-ACDs could not disrupt the free space of the polymer chains or affect the torsion of the water molecular pathways. The small size of CDs cannot create complex pathways, and WVP did not significantly (p > 0.05) decrease because WVP depends not only on inter-chain interactions but also on the ability of fillers to impede water vapor passage [36].

Furthermore, the WCA of the CNF/PUL/CD composite films is also displayed in Table 2. The WCA of the neat CNF/PUL film was 59.6°, indicating the hydrophilicity of the control film. When the WCA value of biopolymer films is <65°, they are typically considered hydrophilic [26]. For the prepared composite films, the WCA decreased from 59.6° to 55.2° as the concentration of Zn-ACDs changed from 1 to 5 wt%. The WCA shows that the hydrophilicity of the fabricated films is slightly increased by the loading of CDs. Incorporating the high content of Zn-ACDs (5 wt%) forms a cross-linked network between the polymer matrix and CDs, reducing the effective interaction among the hydrophilic groups of the matrix and water [44]. Slight changes in the WCA showed similar trends to changes in water vapor barrier properties analyzed for the CNF/PUL/CD composite films.

Fig. 5(a) shows the antioxidant activity of Zn-ACDs compared to ascorbic acid (AA) by scavenging DPPH and ABTS free radicals. AA is a powerful antioxidant and is often used as a reference material for its free radical scavenging properties [9]. As a reducing agent, AA reacts as a donor using a single reducing equivalent (H or H+ + e−) cycling between fully reduced AA and the radical anion, monodehydroascorbate. It is well known that AA can scavenge 100 % DPPH and ABTS free radicals in a concentration-dependent manner, which makes AA an excellent free radical scavenger. Therefore, in this study, AA was compared Zn-ACDs and AA to determine dose-dependent ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities.

The results show the radical scavenging action of Zn-ACDs is dose-dependent and substantially (p < 0.05) improved with increasing concentration from 12.5 to 100 g/mL. The Zn-ACDs demonstrated remarkable antioxidant activities, and they exhibited 45.8 ± 1 % and 16.5 ± 0.8 % activities of the ABTS and DPPH methods at a low concentration of 12.5 μg/mL, respectively. The CDs displayed 100 % ABTS and 81.5 % DPPH radicals quenching at 100 g/mL. The antioxidant activity of Zn-ACDs was comparable to AA, confirming the acceptable antioxidant performance of Zn-ACDs. The hydrophilic nature of Zn-ACDs results in increased release in polar solvents compared to nonpolar solvents. Hence, the aqueous solution-based ABTS method demonstrates higher antioxidant activity for Zn-ACDs than the methanolic-based DPPH method [45]. The radical scavenging action of CDs is attributed to the H-donating behavior of edge site functionalities such as -OH, -COOH, and -NH2. Furthermore, their electron donation promotes free radicals' destruction, which leads to the delocalization and stabilization of free electrons in the sp2-hybridized carbon network and the formation of the adduct [46,47]. The presence of metal dopants of Zn can further enhance the antioxidant function of ACDs by impacting their electron transfer properties [48].

The antioxidant activity of the CNF/PUL/CD films is presented in Fig. 5(b). The neat CNF/PUL film did not exhibit any radical scavenging activity in either method, whereas the composite films exhibited free radical scavenging activity dependent on CD concentration. The CNF/PUL/CD with 2, 3, and 5 wt% Zn-ACDs demonstrated 100 % antioxidant potential in the ABTS and 33, 47, and 68 % in the DPPH technique, respectively. A higher scavenging activity measured using the ABTS technique is due to the hydrophilic nature of CNF/PUL blended polymer film, which facilitates the release of the active ingredient into the ABTS aqueous solution, leading to improved interactions between ABTS•+ and the hydrophilic Zn-ACDs, as compared to the DPPH methanolic solution [26]. The combination of hydrogen donation, electron transfer, and radical adduct formation provided by Zn-ACDs induces the ABTS• or DPPH• scavenging for the composite films.

Microbial food spoilage is a significant issue for society. Bacteria and fungi can spoil perishable foods and cause alterations in food qualities such as taste, texture, color, odor, and flavor [49]. Antimicrobial packaging has emerged as a promising active packaging technology to inhibit and restrict microbial growth and increase food safety and shelf life [49]. The antimicrobial efficacy of Zn-ACDs was studied against L. monocytogenes and E. coli as the common food pathogenic bacteria. The antimicrobial activity was determined as MIC and MBC, and the Zn-ACDs displayed MIC/MBC values of 400/600 μg/mL and 200/400 μg/mL against L. monocytogenes and E. coli, respectively. In addition, the antibacterial activity of CDs was qualitatively evaluated for both strains according to the agar well diffusion method, and the results are displayed in Fig. 6 (a-d). The neat broth (control) showed no growth inhibition for either bacterial strains as shown in Fig. 6(a) & (c). However, the Zn-ACDs solution exerted a strong inhibitory effect against both L. monocytogenes and E. coli strains and showed better activity against E. coli in particular. As a result, the hydrophilic Zn-ACDs rapidly diffuse and disperse into the agar medium, resulting in a bactericidal effect [26]. The well-diffusion test findings match the MIC results.

The principles behind the antibacterial properties of Zn-ACDs are as follows: (i) Physical destruction: in this mechanism, the hydrogen bonding or electrostatic contact between functional groups present at the surround of carbon dots and porins, peptidoglycans, and proteins in bacterial cell membranes can cause the breakdown of the cell wall and bio-membrane and release of the intracellular components [50,51]. (ii) Oxidative stress: the oxidative stress caused by the reactive oxygen species (ROS) production is another significant mechanism for the antibacterial action. Bacterial growth can be hampered by ROS, including singlet oxygen, hydroxyl free radicals, and superoxide anions, through damaging effects on nucleic acids, inactivating proteins, and inducing lipid peroxidation [52–54]. (iii) Size effect: the distribution of nano-sized Zn-ACDs within the membrane structure is strongly influenced by their size, and the bacteria cell wall is permeable to the small nanoparticles [55].

In addition, metal ions (zinc ions) doped in ACDs enhance the antibacterial activity by facilitating the electron transfer process and enhancing the rate of ROS production. Similarly, various metal ions, including Ag+, Fe3+, etc., can generate ROS through chemical reactions inside cells [50]. These ROS damage nucleic acids to inhibit bacterial growth, cause lipid peroxidation, and inactivate proteins. Also, zinc ions are released during interaction with microorganisms and taken up into cells, where they can interact with cellular proteins and nucleic acids, disrupting their normal functions and eventually leading to cell death [11].

The antimicrobial activity of CNF/PUL/CD composite films against L. monocytogenes and E. coli is illustrated in Fig. 6(e), (f). The antibacterial action against L. monocytogenes and E. coli was not observed in the pure CNF/PUL film. In contrast, the film samples containing Zn-ACDs demonstrated concentration-dependent antibacterial activity against tested bacteria. The CNF/PUL/CD1.0 and CNF/PUL/CD2.0 composite films showed bacteriostatic effects against both microorganisms; however, the composite films with 3 and 5 wt% CDs showed remarkable bactericidal effect and completely hindered the growth of L. monocytogenes and E. coli. The remarkable antibacterial activities of the CNF/PUL/CD composite films can be attributed to the antibacterial properties of the Zn-ACDs, which have been described in detail above.

The effect of the designed composite films on consumer visual scores (overall acceptability) in an in vitro model system of chicken breast and tofu was monitored for a storage period of 9 days at 10 °C (Fig. 7 ). Visually, the surface color of the neat CNF/PUL-wrapped chicken breast darkened over time during storage, bleaching the chicken's original red color (Fig. 7(a)). Color change in chicken samples during storage is due to microbial food spoilage, which converts the red pigment in raw chicken meat to brown metmyoglobin [56]. No significant differences existed between tofu appearances wrapped in different film formulations (Fig. 7(b)).

The TBARS values related to the degree of lipid oxidation of samples were calculated over time, and the results are presented in Fig. 8 (a,b). The initial TBA level in the chicken wrapped with CNF/PUL was 0.21 mg of MDA/kg of chicken meat, which exceeded 2.11 mg at the end of keeping time. The TBARS values showed an upward trend in the control group, whereas samples packaged by CNF/PUL/CD films had lower TBARS values during storage.

For the tofu samples, the initial TBA level in the control treatment was 0.52 mg of MDA/kg tofu, which exceeded 1.42 mg at the end of keeping time. On the 9th day, the TBARS formation in the samples treated by CNF/PUL/CD was significantly lower than in the control group (p < 0.05). This change could be due to the antioxidant activity of the carbon dots and the impact of these agents on lipid oxidation. Zhao et al. [57] revealed that the polyvinyl alcohol composite film incorporating carbon dots prolonged the shelf life of fruits and meatballs by its radical scavenging activity. These results indicated that CNF/PUL/CD composite films efficiently inhibited lipid oxidation and could be a potential packaging material.

Alterations in the pH of chicken and tofu samples packaged with CNF/PUL-based composite films are presented in Fig. 8(c,d). The primary pH of chicken and tofu was 5.68 and 6.05, and the pH values of all groups were enhanced during the storage by the action of microbial or endogenous enzymes like lipase and protease that caused an increase in the levels of trimethylamine and ammonia in the package [58]. However, the pH values of the samples wrapped in the CNF/PUL/CD composite film were consistently lower than the other samples during the entire storage period. Elevated pH level has a negative effect on the quality of the sample, particularly affecting its organoleptic properties such as color, odor, and texture. It can be emphasized that the CNF/PUL/CD film exhibits stronger antibacterial activity compared to the CNF/PUL film. Therefore, the high microbial growth rate of the samples covered with the CNF/PUL control film during storage could be associated with their higher pH. These results are consistent with those of Chen et al. [59] that combining chitosan and carbon dot can significantly reduce the pH value of packaged pork.

The effect of the designed composite films on the growth inhibition of L. monocytogenes and E. coli bacteria is presented in Fig. 8(e). Remarkable differences are observed in the antimicrobial efficacy of the composite films in the model samples. In chicken and tofu wrapped in the neat CNF/PUL film, L. monocytogenes, and E. coli rapidly grew (no antimicrobial effect), whereas growth was significantly (p < 0.05) reduced in the CNF/PUL/CD composite films. The growth inhibitory effect increased with increasing Zn-ACDs concentration. The pathogen population was completely eradicated from samples wrapped with CNF/PUL/CD5 composite film, confirming the antibacterial function of the designed film against foodborne pathogens. Therefore, the formulated CNF/PUL/CD composite film is a sustainable and safe packaging option for protecting food products from microbial contamination.

A multifunctional active packaging film with excellent antimicrobial activity was obtained using a CNF/PUL matrix impregnated with Zn-ACDs prepared using avocado as a precursor. The TEM micrograph validated the spherical diameter of the CDs (~2.1 to 5.9 nm) and verified the distribution of CDs in the quantum range. It has been found that Zn-ACDs can significantly improve the physical properties of composite films. FTIR and SEM analysis show that Zn-ACDs are compatible with the CNF/PUL matrix. The composite film with 5 wt% of Zn-ACDs had the highest physicochemical and functional properties. Zn-ACDs are cost-effective and eco-friendly, give the CNF/PUL-based films functionality, improve tensile strength by 45.4 %, significantly improve ABTS radical scavenging by approximately 100.0 %, DPPH radical scavenging by 71.4 %, and UV-barrier ability. CNF/PUL/CD5 composite film successfully eradicated L. monocytogenes and E. coli after 3 h of exposure. CNF/PUL/CD5 composite film improved the shelf-life of wrapped chicken breast and tofu for 9 days storage at 10 °C with the lowest bacterial counts without significantly altering the visual quality of the wrapped foods. Therefore, CNF/PUL/CD5 composite film has the potential to be employed as active packaging materials for various foods, which is important to achieve high value-added utilization of biomass materials and to meet the demand for environmentally friendly sustainable development.

Zohreh Riahi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ajahar Khan: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing. Gye Hwa Shin: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jun Tae Kim: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
